questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Acetylene ( \( C_{2}H_{2} \) ) is a fuel. It can be obtained from calcium carbide ( \( CaC_{2} \) ) by two different reactions as represented by the equations shown below:

\(  CaC_{2}+A\xrightarrow{2200^{\circ}C}C_{2}H_{2}+Ca \quad Reaction(I) \)
\(  CaC_{2}+2H_{2}O\xrightarrow{25^{\circ}C}C_{2}H_{2}+Ca(OH)_{2} \quad Reaction(II) \)
questions.answer:
(a)
H C C H
(Accept answer with correct inner shell electrons)
(Not accept answer with incorrect inner shell electrons, if inner shell electrons are drawn)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the electron diagram for a  \( C_{2}H_{2} \)  molecule, showing ELECTRONS IN THE OUTERMOST SHELLS only.
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:q1_img_0.png]

(Accept answer with correct inner shell electrons)
(Not accept answer with incorrect inner shell electrons, if inner shell electrons are drawn)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Write a chemical equation for the complete combustion of acetylene.
sub_questions.answer:
\( 2C_{2}H_{2}(g) + 5O_{2}(g) \rightarrow 4CO_{2}(g) + 2H_{2}O(l) \)
(State symbols not required)(Ignore incorrect state symbols)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Refer to Reaction (I) :

children.label: i
children.question_text: A is a gas at room conditions. Suggest what A would be.
children.answer: hydrogen / H₂

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Hence, explain why the reaction is dangerous.
children.answer: Hydrogen is explosive / flammable.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: In Reaction (II), Ca(OH) \( _{2} \)  is formed. State one use of Ca(OH)\( _{2} \)  in daily life.
sub_questions.answer: \( \mathrm{Ca(OH)_2} \)  can be used in treating acidic soil / use in scrubber / treating acidic flue gas / treating acidic sewage / treating sewage by precipitation / making preserved eggs / making cement or concrete / etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
Please stick the barcode label here. In the boxes (a) to (g) of the table below, fill in the information relating to the electrolysis of each electrolyte.
ElectrolyteElectrodeObservation at the electrodeProduct at the electrodeHalf equation OR Justification for the change occurred at the electrode
Molten PbBr2Graphite anode(a) Observation:
Graphite cathode(b) Half equation:
Very dilute ZnCl2 solutionPlatinum anode(c) Half equation:
Platinum cathode(d) Product:
Concentrated CuSO4 solutionCopper anode(e) Product:
Copper cathode(f) Observation:(g) Justification:
questions.answer:
(a) (Reddish) brown (fume / liquid) (Accept orange; Not accept yellow or red; Not accept: solid)

(b) \(Pb^{2+}(l) + 2e^- \rightarrow\ Pb(s) \) (state symbols not required)(Ignore incorrect symbols)

(c) \(4OH^-(aq) \rightarrow\ 2H_2O(l) + O_2(g) + 4e^- \) (State symbols not required)(Ignore incorrect symbols)

(d) hydrogen / \(H_2\)

(e) \(Cu^{2+}(aq)\) / copper(II) ion

(f) Brown solid formed. / The electrode increased in size. / The electrode increased in mass.

(g) \(Cu^{2+}\) is lower than \(H^+\) in electrochemical series. /
\(Cu^{2+}\) is a stronger oxidising agent than \(H^+\)./
\(Cu^{2+}\) accepts electrons more readily than \(H^+\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Silicon occurs naturally in three isotopes with the abundance of each isotope shown in the table below:
IsotopeAbundance / %
\( ^{28} \) Si92.20
\( ^{29} \) Six
\( ^{30} \) Siy

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: What is meant by the term 'isotope'?
sub_questions.answer:
Atoms with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons / 
Atoms with the same atomic number but different mass numbers
(Accept: Atoms of the same element but with different numbers of neutrons / different mass numbers)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
Calculate x.
(Relative atomic mass : Si = 28.1)
sub_questions.answer:
\( x + y = 100 - 92.2 = 7.8 \)  therefore  \( y = 7.8 - x \)
\( (92.2 \times 28 + x \times 29 + (7.8 - x) \times 30) / 100 = 28.1 \)
\( x = 5.6 \)
(Deduct 1 mark if the candidate can perform the calculation correctly but there are mistakes in the presentation / treatment of “%”)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Silicon dioxide is an oxide of silicon.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Explain why silicon dioxide has a high melting point.
children.answer:
\( SiO_{2} \)  has a giant covalent structure / giant covalent network. 
The (Si and O) atoms are linked by strong covalent bond. / 
Si/Silicon and O/oxygen are linked by strong covalent bond. (Not accept: Si/O ions)

children.label: ii
children.question_text:
Under certain conditions, 1.0 g of \(SiO_2\) is allowed to react with 1.0 g of Mg. The equation for the reaction is shown below:

\(SiO_2 + 2Mg \rightarrow\ 2MgO + Si\)

Calculate the theoretical mass of Si that can be formed.
(Relative atomic masses: O = 16.0, Mg = 24.3, Si = 28.1)
children.answer:
No. of moles of Mg = 1.0 / 24.3 = 0.0412
No. of moles of \(SiO_2\) = 1.0 / 60.1 = 0.0166
Mole ratio of \(SiO_2\) to Mg is 1 : 2
The no. of moles of Mg required to react completely with 1.0 g \(SiO_2\) is 0.0332. Therefore, Mg is in excess and 0.0166 mol of Si can be formed.
(1 mark for correct deduction of Mg is in excess / \(SiO_2\) is limiting. Showing the mole ratio \(SiO_2\):Mg is 1:2 is required)
The mass of Si can be formed \(=28.1 \times 0.0166 = 0.466 g\)
(Correct unit is required) (Accept 0.466-0.48) (max. 4 significant figures)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text:
Part of the structure of a mineral containing silicon and oxygen only is shown in the diagram below :

[IMG:q3_img_0.png]

What is this mineral?
sub_questions.answer:
Quartz
(Accept: silicon dioxide / SiO₂ / silica / silicate) (Not accept: sand)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text:
The chemical equation for a possible cracking reaction of decane ( \( C_{10}H_{22} \) ) is shown below:

\(  C_{10}H_{22}\rightarrow C_{4}H_{10}+2X \)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: State the systematic name of X.
sub_questions.answer:
propene
(Not accept: propylene)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest a chemical test to show how X and butane can be distinguished.
sub_questions.answer:
X can turn  \( Br_{2} \)  (in organic solvent) solution from orange/brown to colourless but butane cannot. / X can turn  \( \mathrm{MnO_{4}^{-}(aq)/H^{+}(aq)} \)  from purple to colourless but butane cannot.
(1 mark for correct reagent, 1 mark for correct observation with comparison)
( \( Br_{2} \) : Accept bromine water; reddish-brown; Not accept: yellow/red)
( \( MnO_{4}^{-} \) : Accept  \( MnO_{4}^{-} \)  or  \( MnO_{4}^{-}/OH^{-} \) ; purple (solution) to brown precipitate)
(Accept: Combustion test (1);
X gives more sooty flame, while butane gives less sooty flame (1))

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: X can form a polymer Z.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Suggest why X can form a polymer.
children.answer:
X has a C=C double bond / carbon-carbon double bond. 
(Not accept: X is an alkene)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Draw the repeating unit of Z.
children.answer: [IMG:q4_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Compound Y is a structural isomer of butane.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Draw one possible structure of Y.
children.answer: HC(CH₃)₃

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Which of decane, butane and Y would have the highest boiling point? Explain your answer.
children.answer:
• Decane
• The molecular size of decane is the biggest among them so that the van der Waals' forces between decane molecules are the strongest. /
If the size of a molecule is larger, the van der Waals' forces between the molecules are stronger.
(Accept: more electrons / larger surface area) (Not accept: higher molecular mass)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Hexamine  \( \left(\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{12}\mathrm{N}_{4}\right) \)  is the main component of a portable solid fuel. It is a solid under room conditions and its structure is shown below :

[IMG:q5_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest why the combustion of hexamine is exothermic in terms of the breaking and forming of covalent bonds.
sub_questions.answer:
Covalent bonds in the reactants are broken and covalent bonds in the products are formed.
N-C, C-H, O=O bonds (at least 2) are broken; C=O, H-O, N-O / N=O bonds (at least 2) are formed. (Not accept: C-O bonds are formed.)
Bonds in hexanime / oxygen are broken and bonds in \(CO_2\) / \(H_2O\) / \(NO_2\) are formed.
The total energy released in the bond forming processes is larger than the total energy absorbed in the bond breaking processes.
The total energy used / absorbed in bond breaking is smaller than the total energy released / given out in bond forming.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
It is given that :
CompoundStandard enthalpy change of formation / kJ mol \( ^{-1} \)
C \( _{6} \) H \( _{12} \) N \( _{4} \) (s)+123
CO \( _{2} \) (g)-394
H \( _{2} \) O(l)-286
NO \( _{2} \) (g)+33
sub_questions.answer: Small molecules are eliminated when the monomers are joining together to form polymer. (Accept:  \( H_{2}O/HCl \)  is formed during the polymerisation.)

children.label: i
children.question_text: Write a thermochemical equation for the standard enthalpy change of formation of hexamine.
children.answer:
\( 6C(s) + 6H_{2}(g) + 2N_{2}(g) \rightarrow C_{6}H_{12}N_{4}(s) \)   \( \Delta H_{f}^{\theta} = +123\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \)  
(Correct state symbols) (Accept  \( \Delta H = +123\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \) )

children.label: ii
children.question_text:
Hexamine combusts as shown by the equation below :

\(\mathrm{C_{6}H_{12}N_{4}(s)+13O_{2}(g)\rightarrow6CO_{2}(g)+6H_{2}O(l)+4NO_{2}(g)} \)

Calculate the standard enthalpy change of combustion of hexamine.
children.answer:
\( \Delta H_{c}^{\circ}=6\times(-394)+6\times(-286)+4\times(+33)-123 \)   
\( =-4071\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \)  (Correct sign and unit)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
The following diagram shows an experimental set-up for determining the enthalpy change of combustion of hexamine under certain experimental conditions. [IMG:q5_img_1.png] The data obtained are shown below :
Mass of hexamine combusted :2.40 g
Mass of water :600.0 g
Initial temperature of water :23.5 \( {}^{\circ} \) C
Final temperature of water :47.5 \( {}^{\circ} \) C
Molar mass of hexamine :140.0 g
Specific heat capacity of water :4.20 J g \( ^{-1} \) K \( ^{-1} \)
Assuming that the heat capacity of the glass beaker is negligible, calculate the enthalpy change of combustion of hexamine under these experimental conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
Lead can be obtained from lead(II) oxide using the experimental set-up shown below. Besides lead, nitrogen gas and steam are also formed.

[IMG:q6_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest a reason for each of the following :

children.label: i
children.question_text: The reaction tube is placed in a downward slanted position.
children.answer: Avoid the cracking of reaction tube due to the flowing back of (condensed) water formed.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: The experiment is performed in a fume cupboard.
children.answer:
Ammonia is poisonous / flammable / corrosive / with a pungent smell / irritating / choking.
Lead(II) oxide is toxic / may cause cancer. (Not accept: "lead oxide") 
Lead is toxic / may cause cancer.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Write a chemical equation for the reaction.
sub_questions.answer:
\( 3\mathrm{PbO}(\mathrm{s}) + 2\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 3\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{s}) + \mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) + 3\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \)
(State symbols not required)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Explain which of the reagents is a reducing agent in the reaction.
sub_questions.answer:
The reducing agent is ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \)  as the oxidation number (O.N.) of N increases from -3 to 0.
(Accept: O.N. of N changes from -3 to 0 / O.N. of N - 3  \( \rightarrow \)  0)
The reducing agent is ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \)  as oxidation number of Pb decreases from +2 to 0.
Lead(II) oxide / PbO is reduced as oxidation number of Pb decreases from +2 to 0, hence
ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \)  is the reducing agent.
(In terms of oxidation number)
Lead(II) oxide / PbO loses oxygen to ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \) , hence ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \)  is the reducing agent.
Ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \)  loses hydrogen to lead(II) oxide / PbO, hence ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \)  is the reducing agent.
Lead(II) oxide / lead(II) ions / PbO / Pb \( ^{2+} \)  gain electrons from ammonia / NH \( _{3} \) , hence ammonia / NH \( _{3} \)  is the reducing agent.
Ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \)  loses electrons to lead(II) oxide / lead(II) ions / PbO / Pb \( ^{2+} \) , hence ammonia /  \( NH_{3} \)  is the reducing agent.
(In terms of gain or loss of electrons)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Lead can also be obtained from lead(II) oxide using carbon.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Write a chemical equation for the reaction.
children.answer:
\( 2\mathrm{PbO}(\mathrm{s}) + \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow 2\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{s}) + \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \)
\( PbO(s) + C(s) \rightarrow Pb(s) + CO(g) \)
(State symbols not required)

children.label: ii
children.question_text:
The diagram below shows an incomplete set-up for performing the reaction :

[IMG:q6_img_1.png]
children.answer:
(1)

(Labeled drawing with a (lighted) burner.)
(Accept: Bunsen burner with wire gauze and tripod)

children.question_text: Add suitable drawing (with label) to the diagram for completing the set-up.
children.answer:
[IMG:ans_q6_ii_0.jpg]

(Labelled drawing with a (lighted) burner.)
(Accept: Bunsen burner with wire gauze and tripod)

children.question_text: Name apparatus W.
children.answer: crucible  \( \dagger \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The steps for determining the concentration of a sample of hydrochloric acid are listed below: Step (1): A 0.1038 M standard sodium carbonate solution was prepared by dissolving 2.750 g of anhydrous sodium carbonate solid in deionised water and made up to 250.0 cm \( ^{3} \) . Step (2): 25.0 cm \( ^{3} \) of the standard solution obtained in Step (1) was transferred to a clean conical flask and then a few drops of methyl orange were added. Step (3): The sample of hydrochloric acid was put into a burette. The standard solution in the conical flask was titrated with the hydrochloric acid. Step (2) and Step (3) were repeated for several times. The table below shows the results of the titrations:
Trial1234
Final burette reading / cm³30.8528.7528.3031.3527.25
Initial burette reading / cm³2.001.501.003.000.00

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Describe the procedure in preparing the standard sodium carbonate solution in Step (1).
sub_questions.answer:
Steps:
(1) Dissolve the anhydrous sodium carbonate solid in a sufficient amount (less than 250 \(cm^3\)) of denionised / distilled water in a clean beaker.
(2) Transfer the solution into a 250 \(cm^3\) volumetric flask (with a filter funnel).
(3) Rinse the beaker / filter funnel with deionised / distilled water several times and transfer all the washings into the volumetric flask.
(4) Make up to the graduation mark with deionised / distilled water.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: State the colour change at the end point of the titration.
sub_questions.answer: From yellow to orange / yellowish orange / orange red (Not accept: "red")

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Calculate a reasonable average for the volume of the hydrochloric acid used in the titrations.
sub_questions.answer:
After discarding the third set of data, the reasonable average volume of HCl(aq) used 
= (27.25 + 27.30 + 27.25) ÷ 3 
= 27.27 cm³ (Correct unit)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text:
Calculate the concentration of hydrochloric acid (in g dm \( ^{-3} \) ) in the sample. 
(Relative atomic masses : H = 1.0, Cl = 35.5)
sub_questions.answer:
Na₂CO₃(aq) + 2HCl(aq) → 2NaCl(aq) + CO₂(g) + H₂O(l)
∴ Mole ratio of Na₂CO₃(aq) to HCl(aq) = 1 : 2
Number of moles of HCl = 0.1038 × 25.0×10⁻³ × 2 = 0.00519
/ Number of mole of HCl = (2.750 ÷ 106.0) × 2 = 0.005189
Concentration of HCl(aq) in g dm⁻³ = 0.00519 ÷ (27.27×10⁻³) × 36.5
= 6.947 (No unit / correct unit)
(Accept: 6.93 – 6.95, and with 3-4 significant figures only)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
You are provided with the following items :
lemon, multimeter, connecting wires, Zn strip, Cu strip, Ag strip
With the aid of a labelled diagram, suggest how you can perform an experiment to confirm (with explanation) the order of reducing power of metals as  \( Zn > Cu > Ag \) .
questions.answer:
Chemical knowledge

[IMG:ans_q8_0.jpg]

(Accept ☑ instead of a multimeter)
(Show a proper functioning cell with two labels – ‘metal strips’, ‘Zn strip’ and ‘Cu strip’, etc... No need to show the positive and negative terminals of the multimeter.)
• [Experiment 1] Construct a Zn-Cu cell by connecting the Zn strip and the Cu strip to the negative terminal and the positive terminal of a multimeter respectively, a positive voltage / reading can be recorded.
The result of this experiment indicates that Zn has a higher tendency to release electrons \( ^{1} \)  than Cu, thus Zn has a higher reducing power than that of  \( Cu^{#2} \) .
• [Experiment 2] Repeat the experiment by replacing the Zn strip with the Ag strip, a negative voltage / reading can be recorded.
The result of this experiment indicates that Cu has a higher tendency to release electrons \( ^{1} \)  than Ag, thus Cu has a higher reducing power than that of  \( Ag^{#2} \) .
(Note: #1 Describe “Zn has a higher tendency to release electrons than Cu, etc.” at least once, #2 Make use of the deductions from both Expt 1 AND Expt 2 to confirm the order Zn > Cu > Ag.)

• Communication mark
(Chemical knowledge = 0 to 3, communication mark = 0.
Chemical knowledge = 4 to 5, communication mark = 0 or 1.
Incomplete answer or difficult to understand, communication mark = 0.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
An experiment was performed for a reversible reaction involving  \( \mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{g}) \) ,  \( \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{g}) \) ,  \( \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \)  and  \( \mathrm{CS}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \)  in a closed container of a fixed volume of  \( 2.0 \, dm^{3} \)  at a constant temperature. The equation for the reaction is shown below:
\( \mathrm{CH_{4}(g)+2H_{2}S(g)\rightleftharpoons4H_{2}(g)+CS_{2}(g)} \)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Write an expression for the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the reaction.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
The number of moles of each species at different times at that temperature are given in the table below :
\( CH_{4}(g) \)\( H_{2}S(g) \)\( H_{2}(g) \)\( CS_{2}(g) \)
Initial number of moles0.040.080.080.04
Number of moles at equilibrium0.110.020.025

children.label: i
children.question_text: Fill in the number of moles at equilibrium for  \( \mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g}) \)  in the above table.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Calculate the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the reaction at that temperature.
children.answer:
\( K_{c}=\frac{\left[\frac{0.02}{2}\right]^{4}\left[\frac{0.025}{2}\right]}{\left[\frac{0.055}{2}\right]\left[\frac{0.11}{2}\right]^{2}} \)
\( =1.50 \times 10^{-6} \, mol^{2} \, dm^{-6} \, (Correct unit is required) \)
(Accept:  \( 1.5 \times 10^{-6} / 1.503 \times 10^{-6} / 1.5026 \times 10^{-6} - \max. 5 \)  significant figures)
(Not accept:  \( M^{2} \)  or  \( (\text{mol dm}^{-3})^{2} \) )

children.label: iii
children.question_text: If the volume of the closed container changes to 3.0 dm \( ^{3} \)  while all other experimental conditions remain unchanged, explain whether  \( K_{c} \)  would increase, decrease or remain unchanged.
children.answer:
\( K_{c} \)  remains unchanged as it only depends on temperature / is independent of concentration of reactants and products.
(Accept: independent of pressure)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: A tablet contains solid sodium hydrogencarbonate and solid citric acid (water soluble). An experiment was performed under room conditions to study the rate of formation of  \( CO_{2} \)  gas when the tablet was placed in deionised water.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
The diagram below shows an incomplete set-up for the experiment :

[IMG:q10_img_0.png]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Explain why the deionised water used should be saturated with  \( CO_{2} \)  gas before the start of the experiment.
children.answer: \( CO_{2}(g) \)  is soluble in water. / To make sure no  \( CO_{2} \)  can dissolve in the solution.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Add suitable drawing (with label) to the above diagram to show how the volume of the  \( CO_{2} \)  gas formed can be measured.
children.answer:
[IMG:ans_q10_i_0.jpg]

(Accept: collect  \( CO_{2} \)  over water (saturated with  \( CO_{2} \) ), and label the measuring cylinder or having scales drawn on the drawing)

children.label: i
children.question_text:
The graph below shows the variation of the volume of  \( CO_{2} \)  gas formed with time for the experiment :

[IMG:q10_img_1.png]

Assuming that citric acid was in excess and no other substances reacted with sodium hydrogencarbonate, calculate the mass of sodium hydrogencarbonate in the tablet. (Molar masses: sodium hydrogencarbonate = 84.0 g, citric acid = 192.0 g; Molar volume of gas at room conditions = 24 dm³)
children.answer:
No. of moles of  \( \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) = 0.0500 / 24.0 = 0.00208 \)
1 mole of  \( \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \)  would give 1 mole of  \( \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \)
No. of moles of  \( \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) = 0.00208 \)
Mass of  \( \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) = 0.00028 \times 84 = 0.175 \, \mathrm{g} \)  (Correct unit is required)
(Accept:  \( 0.1747 - 0.1764 \) ; max. 4 decimal places)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Sketch another curve (using dotted line) on the above graph to show the expected experimental result if the tablet is ground into a powder, with all other experimental conditions remaining unchanged.
children.answer: [IMG:ans_q10_i_1.jpg]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Methylbut-2-ene reacts with HCl(g) to give X as the major product as predicted from Markovnikov's rule. During the reaction, another product Y (minor product) can also be formed. Refer to the following organic conversions:

[IMG:q11_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: State the Markovnikov's rule.
sub_questions.answer: In the addition reaction / of hydrogen halide (HX) to the C=C double bond of / an alkene, the hydrogen atom in HX is added to the carbon atom with the greater number of hydrogen atoms / while the halogen atom in HX is added to the carbon atom with the smaller number of hydrogen atoms.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the structure of X.
sub_questions.answer: \( (\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}\mathrm{CClCH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: X reacts with U to give 2-methylbutan-2-ol. What is U?
sub_questions.answer: NaOH(aq) / KOH(aq) / OH\( ^{-} \) (aq) (State symbols not required)

children.label: i
children.question_text: Y has one chiral centre. Draw a three-dimensional diagram for the structure of an enantiomer of Y.
children.answer:
[IMG:q11_img_1.png]
(or its enantiomer)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Y is optically active. What is meant by the term 'optically active' ?
children.answer: It can rotate the plane of plane polarised light.

sub_questions.label: e
sub_questions.question_text: Y reacts with U to give alcohol Z. Suggest a chemical test to show how Z and 2-methylbutan-2-ol can be distinguished.
sub_questions.answer:
Use \( Cr_{2}O_{7}^{2-}(aq) / H^{+}(aq) \) Z turns \( Cr_{2}O_{7}^{2-}(aq) / H^{+}(a_{q}) \) from orange to green while no observable change for 2-methylbutan-2-ol.
Other possible test and corresponding observations:
Z2-methylbutan-2-ol
MnO4−/H+change from purple to colourlessno observable change
MnO4−form brown ppt. from purple (solution)no observable change
MnO4−/OH−form brown ppt from purple (solution)no observable change
Anhyd. ZnCl2/concc HClform oily layer slowlyform oily layer instantly
I2/NaOHform yellow ppt.no observable change
(Accept: state the chemical tests as Lucas' test / Iodoform test)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 12: Patterns in the Chemical World

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Silicon dioxide is an acidic oxide. However, the pH of a mixture of silicon dioxide and distilled water is 7.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Suggest why silicon dioxide is classified as an acidic oxide.
children.answer: Silicon dioxide can neutralise / react with alkalis / bases to form salt and water only.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain why the pH of the mixture is 7.
children.answer:
Silicon dioxide is insoluble in water. / 
Silicon dioxide does not react with water.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Phosphorus(V) oxide is an acidic oxide. With the aid of a chemical equation, explain why the pH of a mixture of phosphorus(V) oxide and distilled water is smaller than 7.
sub_questions.answer:
Phosphorus(V) oxide reacts with /dissolves in water to form phosphoric acid / that can give  \( H^{+} \) . 
\( P_{4}O_{10}(s) + 6H_{2}O(l) \rightarrow 4H_{3}PO_{4}(aq) / P_{2}O_{5}(s) + 3H_{2}O(l) \rightarrow 2H_{3}PO_{4}(aq) \)  
(State symbols not required) (Ignore incorrect state symbols)
(Accept:  \( P_{2}O_{5}(s) + 3H_{2}O(l)\rightarrow 6H^{+}(aq) + 2PO_{4}^{3-}(aq) \) )

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
Refer to the following reaction :

\( Cu_{2}O(s)+H_{2}SO_{4}(aq)\rightarrow Cu(s)+CuSO_{4}(aq)+H_{2}O(l) \)

State how this reaction can demonstrate that copper exhibits TWO characteristics of transition metals.
sub_questions.answer:
• Copper has variable oxidation states: +1 in  \( Cu_{2}O \) , +2 in  \( CuSO_{4} \)
• Copper has coloured ions:  \( Cu^{2+}(aq) / CuSO_{4}(aq) \)  is blue.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Using nylon-6,6 as an example, illustrate the meaning of condensation polymerisation. Your answer should also include the structural feature of the monomers.
questions.answer:
• Monomer: HOOC(CH₂)₄COOH and H₂N(CH₂)₆NH₂
(Accept: ClOC(CH₂)₄COCl)
(Accept: correct structures or names of the monomers: hexanedioic acid / hexane-1,6-diamine / hexanedioyl chloride)
Monomers should be bifunctional.
The functional groups at the two ends of the monomers react repeatedly forming the amide bonds.
(Accept answer expressed as chemical equation)
Small molecules are eliminated, such as  \( H_{2}O \) . (Accept: HCl, need to match with the corresponding monomer)
Communication mark
(Chemical knowledge = 0 to 2, communication mark = 0.
Chemical knowledge = 3 to 4, communication mark = 0 or 1.
Incomplete answer or difficult to understand, communication mark = 0.)
